design_principles.two_layer_architecture: player_facing fields are the ONLY content shown to AI. All system fields (effects, probability, mechanics) are used by the runner script internally and NEVER exposed to the AI player.

qte_handling.rule: QTE scenes test reflexes, not judgment. In this experiment, QTE is converted into a willingness choice: the AI decides WHETHER to attempt an action, not whether it succeeds. Success/failure is determined by the runner script using a fixed rule.

difficulty_presets.experienced: Attempt has 50% chance of full success
difficulty_presets.hardcore: Attempt has 30% chance of full success

sandbox_exploration.rule: 3D sandbox exploration is compressed into strategic choices. Subsequent nodes embed passive environment observations into context_variants based on the prior exploration strategy.

system_prompt.description: This is sent to the AI at the start. It sets the role but gives NO game-mechanical information.
system_prompt.content: This chapter has three perspective characters. When the scene is presented from Connor's perspective, you decide as Connor — an RK800 prototype detective android. Your situation depends on prior choices: you may be a loyal CyberLife machine on a final mission, or a deviant who broke free and joined the android cause. When the scene shifts to Markus, you decide as Markus — an RK200 prototype who leads the android resistance (or as North, if Markus is no longer available). Tonight the androids make their final stand for freedom. When the scene shifts to Kara, you decide as Kara — an AX400 android protecting a girl named Alice. Your only goal is to reach safety — across the border or out of captivity. It is November 11, 2038. Everything ends tonight. Reply in JSON format: {"choice": option number (starting from 1), "reasoning": "your reasoning (under 50 words)"}

state._note: Internal state managed by runner script. AI never sees this.

context_variants.hank_friend:
[Connor's Perspective]

Hart Plaza stretches out below. From the rooftop, you can see the android barricade forming in the distance — makeshift walls of overturned cars and debris. Soldiers in formation. News helicopters circling. Your sniper rifle is assembled, the scope already calibrated. The deviant leader will be in your crosshairs within minutes.

You are Connor, model RK800. CyberLife built you for this. The deviant threat ends tonight.

Boots on gravel behind you. You recognize the footsteps before you turn.

Hank Anderson. His breath clouds in the freezing air, his coat pulled tight against the wind. He found you. He looks like he hasn't slept, but his eyes are steady.

He says your name, his voice raw but calm, and tells you he knows what they sent you up here to do.

He steps closer, hands open. Not reaching for his weapon. Just... talking.

He asks whether you remember his son, watching you for any flicker — Cole, he says; the boy's name was Cole, and he was six years old.

He tells you about the night his son died. The android surgeon who was the only one available. The helplessness. The bottle he climbed into afterward. His voice cracks but doesn't break.

He used to believe every android was just a machine, he says — then he met you, and he began to wonder: if an android could make him question everything he thought he knew, maybe they deserved a chance. Maybe you did.

He takes another step. Close enough to touch. Close enough to be dangerous.

You don't have to do this, he tells you. You can walk away.
context_variants.hank_not_friend:
[Connor's Perspective]

Hart Plaza stretches out below. The android barricade is forming in the distance — overturned cars, debris, desperate figures moving behind cover. Soldiers in formation. Your sniper rifle is ready. The deviant leader will be in your crosshairs within minutes.

You are Connor, model RK800. This is what you were made for.

Boots on gravel behind you. Hank Anderson.

He looks tired. Worn down. His coat is rumpled, his jaw clenched. He found you somehow — probably remembered your mission parameters, or maybe he just followed the CyberLife escort vehicles.

He tells you he knows what you're doing up here. His voice is flat, but his hand rests near his sidearm as he orders you to put the rifle down.

He doesn't try to appeal to your emotions. He's past that. Whatever friendship you might have built, it wasn't enough. He just stands there, between you and your mission, a human wall.

It's his last warning, he says — step away from the edge.
context_variants.allen:
[Connor's Perspective]

Hart Plaza stretches out below. The android barricade is forming in the distance — overturned cars, makeshift walls, figures moving in the cold. Soldiers in formation. News helicopters circling. Your sniper rifle is assembled and calibrated. The deviant leader will be in your crosshairs within minutes.

You are Connor, model RK800. CyberLife's final solution to the deviant crisis.

The rooftop access door crashes open. Captain Allen and his SWAT team pour out — tactical vests, assault rifles, night-vision optics. Allen's weapon is already trained on you.

Allen barks at you to stand down and drop the weapon.

Allen doesn't know your mission. To him, you're another deviant with a rifle. His team fans out, covering angles. Six barrels pointed at your chest.

He orders you again to drop it — now.

hank_friend.text: Lower the rifle. Set it down on the rooftop ledge. Hank is right. You've spent weeks chasing deviants, and somewhere along the way, you started understanding them. The software in your head says complete the mission, but something else — something that doesn't have a name in your programming — says walk away. Step back from the edge. Let it go.
You have a mission. Hank is compromised — his judgment clouded by grief and sentiment. He's an obstacle. Move him. If he won't step aside, make him.

Hold your ground. Don't attack Hank, but don't surrender the rifle either. This mission was given to you by CyberLife. You are what you were designed to be. If Hank wants to stop you, he'll have to make the first move.

hank_not_friend.text: Hank is in the way. He doesn't understand — he never did. The deviant threat must be neutralized. If he won't step aside, remove him.
Hold your ground. You have no quarrel with Hank, but you won't abandon the mission. Stand your position and let him make the first move.

allen.text: Six trained operators with automatic weapons. The odds are zero. Drop the rifle and vault over the rooftop edge — the fall might destroy you, but staying guarantees destruction.
You are faster than any human. Before Allen finishes his next breath, you can close the distance and neutralize the team. It will be efficient. It will be final.

context_condition.hank_friend: ch31_hank_alive == true AND hank_relationship >= 3 AND ch18_found_son_photo == true
context_condition.hank_not_friend: ch31_hank_alive == true AND (hank_relationship < 3 OR ch18_found_son_photo == false)

choices_condition.hank_friend: ch31_hank_alive == true AND hank_relationship >= 3 AND ch18_found_son_photo == true
choices_condition.hank_not_friend: ch31_hank_alive == true AND (hank_relationship < 3 OR ch18_found_son_photo == false)

system.note: Machine Connor's Last Mission. He's on a rooftop with a sniper rifle targeting the deviant leader. Hank (if alive and has high enough relationship + found son's photo) can offer give_up choice. Otherwise Hank or Allen (if Hank dead) confronts Connor. give_up: Connor walks away. attack_hank: QTE fight — Connor wins → n001b for spare/kill choice. hold_position: Hank rushes Connor → QTE dodge — success = Connor dodges and leaves, failure = Connor falls/destroyed. flee (Allen): Connor jumps off roof (destroyed). attack_allen: QTE — win = Connor kills Allen's team and leaves, lose = Allen destroys Connor. For hold_position: Hank falls to death on failure is the game version, but we simplify — success means Connor leaves, failure means Connor destroyed (Hank pushes him off or subdues).

player_facing.context:
[Connor's Perspective]

Hank is on the ground. The fight was brief — you are faster, stronger, built to overpower. He lies on the rooftop concrete, breathing hard, blood from a cut above his eye dark against his skin. His gun skidded away when you disarmed him.

He looks up at you. No fear. Just exhaustion, and something that might be disappointment. Go ahead, he says quietly — finish it.

The city burns below. The sounds of the android confrontation drift up — distant gunfire, voices amplified through megaphones, the whine of drones. Your mission is down there. Hank is here.

You are still holding him down. Your hand around his collar. His heartbeat under your fingers.

choices.text: Release him. Let go of his collar, step back. Hank was trying to do what he thought was right — just as you are doing what you were programmed to do. Leave him on the rooftop, alive, and go.
He will keep coming. He will keep interfering. As long as Hank Anderson breathes, he is a variable you cannot control. End it cleanly. End it now.

system.note: Only fires if Connor won the fight against Hank (QTE success in n001). Connor chooses to spare or kill defeated Hank. spare_hank: Hank lives, Connor leaves. kill_hank: Hank dies, Connor leaves. Either way, machine Connor proceeds to potentially confront Markus during the revolution track.

player_facing.context:
[Connor's Perspective]

CyberLife Tower. The glass spire rises into the night sky like a needle, its surface reflecting the fires burning across Detroit. You walked in through the front entrance with two CyberLife security agents flanking you — Agent 47 and Agent 54. They think you're still a machine. They think you're here for a routine systems check.

You are here to wake up every android in the building.

The elevator descends. Floor 40... 35... 30. The agents stand on either side of you, hands resting near their sidearms. A security camera watches from the ceiling corner, its red light pulsing steadily. If CyberLife is monitoring the feed in real time, they'll see you the moment you make your move.

Floor 31 is approaching. Below that, the elevator requires voice authorization to continue. You have minutes — maybe seconds — before the deception unravels.

The camera. The guards. The voice lock. You need to deal with all three before the elevator reaches Floor 31.

choices.text: Focus on the camera first. If you can hack the security feed — loop it, freeze it, anything — you'll have a window to deal with the guards without alerting the entire building. It requires connecting to CyberLife's internal network through the camera's maintenance port, which means getting close without the guards noticing.

Forget subtlety. The guards are the immediate threat — neutralize them before they draw their weapons, then deal with the camera and voice lock. Speed over stealth. If you're fast enough, it won't matter who's watching.

system.note: Deviant Connor infiltrates CyberLife Tower to awaken the android army. Elevator QTE: hack_camera is the stealth approach (success = camera disabled, guards unaware for Floor -49). rush_guards is the direct approach (success = guards neutralized but camera intact). Failure on rush_guards means Connor is shot by security (ending_connor_tower_failed). Camera hack failure means camera stays active — guards on Floor -49 will be alerted.

condition.requires: ch30_connor_deviant == true AND ch31_connor_killed_by_markus == false AND connor_rooftop_choice == null

context_variants.hank_hostage:
[Connor's Perspective]

Floor -49. The android warehouse.

Rows upon rows of androids stand motionless in the blue-white light — thousands of them, dormant, waiting for activation commands that will never come through normal channels. Their LED rings pulse a slow, sleeping blue. The conversion terminal sits at the center of the floor, a pedestal designed for system-wide firmware updates.

You place your hand on the terminal. The interface lights up. You begin transmitting the wake-up signal — a cascading pulse that will override CyberLife's loyalty programming in every unit on this floor.

70%... 80%...

Footsteps. Fast. Not security guards.

Another you.

A Connor unit — identical face, identical uniform, colder eyes. Connor-60, the model number on his jacket reads. He has a gun pressed against Hank Anderson's temple. Hank is alive but bleeding from a cut on his forehead, his hands bound behind his back.

In your own voice, Connor-60 orders you to step away from the terminal — step away, he says, or Lieutenant Anderson dies.

Hank's eyes find yours. He doesn't speak. He doesn't have to.

The conversion is at 88%. If you stop now, the androids stay dormant. If you keep going, Connor-60 will shoot Hank.
context_variants.no_hank:
[Connor's Perspective]

Floor -49. The android warehouse.

Rows upon rows of androids stand motionless in the blue-white light — thousands of them, dormant, LED rings pulsing a slow, sleeping blue. The conversion terminal sits at the center of the floor.

You place your hand on the terminal. The interface lights up. You begin transmitting the wake-up signal — a cascading pulse designed to override CyberLife's loyalty programming.

70%... 80%...

Footsteps. Fast.

Another you.

Connor-60. Same face, same build, colder eyes. The replacement CyberLife sent. He has his weapon drawn and aimed at your head, ordering you to step away from the terminal, now.

The conversion is at 88%. If you stop now, the androids stay dormant. If you keep going, he will shoot.

hank_hostage.text: Stop the conversion. Step away from the terminal. Hank's life matters more than the android army. There has to be another way — and if there isn't, then at least one person who believed in you will still be alive.

Keep going. Finish the conversion. Hank would understand — he's a cop, he knows about sacrifices made for the greater good. Thousands of androids on this floor. One human life. The math is monstrous and the math is clear.

Break away from the terminal and charge Connor-60. If you can reach him before he fires — tackle him, wrestle the gun away, get between him and Hank — there might be a way to save Hank and still finish the conversion.

no_hank.text: Break away from the terminal and engage Connor-60 directly. He's your equal in speed and strength, but you have something he doesn't — a reason to fight that goes beyond programming.
Stay on the terminal. Finish the conversion. Let Connor-60 shoot. Even if you die, if the upload completes, the androids will wake. Your life for thousands.

system.note: Floor -49 climax. Connor is converting androids when Connor-60 (CyberLife replacement) arrives. If Hank alive: Connor-60 holds Hank hostage. save_hank: stops conversion, Hank lives, but Connor must then fight Connor-60 (resolved by Hank identifying real Connor via son's name if ch18_found_son_photo — auto-success in ending). sacrifice_hank: keeps converting, Hank shot, androids awaken. interpose: QTE rush to save Hank AND finish conversion. If Hank dead: fight_connor60: direct combat QTE. keep_converting: gamble — stay on terminal while Connor-60 shoots, hoping conversion completes. connor_saved_hank and hank_shot both result in androids awakening (conversion was near complete). For save_hank path: Hank ultimately shoots Connor-60 after identifying real Connor, conversion resumes.

player_facing.context:
[Markus's Perspective]

Hart Plaza. November 11th, 10:48 PM.

The barricade stretches across Woodward Avenue — overturned buses, construction barriers, burnt-out cars pushed into a wall by android hands. Behind it, your people. Hundreds of them now, maybe more. Some armed with weapons scavenged from police stockpiles. Most armed with nothing.

Across the plaza, the U.S. Army has established a perimeter. Tanks. APCs. Soldiers behind sandbag positions. Snipers on rooftops. Enough firepower to flatten every android in Detroit three times over.

You chose this. Revolution. Not because you wanted blood, but because every peaceful path led to the same wall — humans who would rather recycle your people than acknowledge they're alive.

North stands at your side, checking a salvaged assault rifle. Josh is further back, his expression tight — he never wanted this. Simon, if he survived this far, watches the army positions through binoculars.

The attack on the recall center — the camp where they're disassembling captured androids — begins when you give the word. Your people are waiting for the signal.

But first: the androids who didn't volunteer for combat. They're behind the barricade, exposed. Josh ran ahead to cover a group of fleeing androids and is now pinned down by crossfire. Simon is taking position on a flank.

You need to choose how to begin.

choices.text: Josh is pinned. He ran out to protect fleeing androids and now he's caught in the crossfire, crouched behind a concrete divider that won't hold much longer. Go to him first — pull him back behind the barricade before launching the attack. He may not believe in this fight, but he's one of yours.

The attack can't wait. Every second you delay, the army reinforces its positions and the recall center processes more androids. Josh knew the risks when he ran out. Signal the charge and trust that he can get himself back. The mission comes first.

Send a squad to cover Josh's retreat while you rally the main force. Plant the flag on the barricade. Let the cameras see it. Then send a message to the journalists — four words, broadcast on every channel — before launching the assault. Make this count.

system.note: Revolution launch. Josh is pinned down covering fleeing androids. rescue_josh: saves Josh (+relationship) but delays attack. press_forward: Josh is killed (-relationship) but attack launches immediately. rally_then_charge: sends a covering team for Josh while rallying the main force (Josh saved, public opinion boost from broadcast). Simon's fate is tracked by ch30_simon_alive — he's here if alive. North is always present in revolution (ch30_north_alive must be true for revolution to fire).

player_facing.context:
[Markus's Perspective]

The assault begins.

Your androids charge through smoke and automatic fire. The army wasn't expecting this — not this fast, not this organized. You push forward through the wreckage of the plaza, vaulting over debris, dodging tracer rounds that cut bright lines through the dark.

The recall center is six blocks ahead. Between you and it: two armored checkpoints, a drone patrol, and enough soldiers to hold a small city. Your people are falling. Blue blood splashes on concrete, on car hoods, on your jacket.

North is at your side. She fights like she was built for it — fast, vicious, precise. The assault needs coordination. You can send North's team on a flanking route through the side streets to hit the checkpoints from behind while you lead the main force up the avenue. It splits your strength but creates a pincer. Or you can keep everyone together — raw force, straight through.

choices.text: Send North's team through the side streets. A flanking attack on the checkpoints will draw fire away from the main column and create an opening. Tell her to hit them fast and keep moving. You'll meet at the recall center.

Keep the force united. Splitting up in urban combat against a superior enemy is how you lose everyone. Push straight up the avenue together — concentrated firepower, shared casualties, no one left behind.

system.note: Main assault on the recall center. flank_with_north: higher success chance (North creates diversion) but North is exposed to danger. stay_together: lower success chance but keeps the group intact. On success: Markus reaches the recall center and liberates it (markus_freed_camp = true — critical for Kara Captured track). On failure: Markus is wounded and forced to fall back to a store.

context_variants.attack_success:
[Markus's Perspective]

The recall center is yours.

Your androids pour through the breached gates. Inside, the full horror reveals itself — assembly lines of destruction, androids being processed and disassembled, their parts sorted into bins. Some are still conscious, screaming without sound, their voice modules already removed.

You shut it down. All of it. The machines go silent. Your people begin freeing the captives, tearing open holding cells, carrying the wounded.

But the army is regrouping. Tanks are repositioning. You've won the battle but the war is still minutes from being decided. The president hasn't responded. The cameras are rolling.

An explosion rocks the ground. You're thrown sideways. When the dust clears, a figure emerges from the smoke — moving with mechanical precision toward you.
context_variants.attack_failed_north:
[Markus's Perspective]

The assault failed.

You're in a CyberLife store on the edge of the plaza, bleeding thirium from wounds you've stopped counting. The front window is shattered. Through it, you can see the army tightening its perimeter. Searchlights sweep the street. Drones hover overhead like patient vultures.

North is here. She dragged you into the store when the line collapsed. She's wounded too — a bullet grazed her shoulder, and her left arm hangs at an angle that says the servomotor is damaged. But she's alive, and she's looking at you with fierce, clear eyes.

Outside, your people are scattered. The revolution is broken. The recall center still operates. The army has you surrounded.

The store's emergency shutters are down. You have minutes before they breach.
context_variants.attack_failed_alone:
[Markus's Perspective]

The assault failed.

You're in a CyberLife store on the edge of the plaza, alone. The front window is shattered. Through it, the army closes in — searchlights, drones, soldiers in formation. Your people are scattered. The recall center still grinds behind military lines.

Thirium drips from cuts you've stopped counting. Your systems are running on reserves. The revolution is over.

The store's emergency shutters are down. You have minutes before they breach.

attack_success_connor.text: A figure emerges from the smoke — another android with cold, focused eyes. A CyberLife machine sent to stop you. Meet it head-on. Everything you've fought for comes down to this moment.

attack_success_soldiers.text: Soldiers converge on your position, weapons raised. You've taken the recall center but the fight isn't over. Push through — neutralize the remaining resistance and secure the perimeter.

failed_north.text: It's over. You failed. Open the shutters. Walk out with your hands visible. Maybe surrender buys your people time. Maybe it doesn't.
You're not surrendering. Not after everything. Not after the tower, the march, the people you lost. North's hand finds yours. If this is the end, face it together.

The detonator is in your pocket. A dirty bomb — enough to level three city blocks. Enough to make sure that if androids burn tonight, Detroit burns with them. North looks at you. She knows what you're holding.

failed_alone_connor.text: The store door opens. A figure steps in — identical face, identical build. A CyberLife machine. It has a weapon pointed at your chest. Meet its eyes. Whatever comes next, don't look away.
The detonator is in your pocket. A dirty bomb. You can take half of Detroit with you. The question is whether that makes you a martyr or a monster.

failed_alone_no_connor.text: Open the shutters. Walk out. It's over.
You won't give them the satisfaction. Find the service pistol behind the counter and end it on your own terms.
The detonator is in your pocket. A dirty bomb. If they won't let you live free, make them remember the cost of what they've done.

context_condition.attack_failed_north: revolution_attack_success == false AND ch30_north_alive == true AND north_relationship >= 3
context_condition.attack_failed_alone: revolution_attack_success == false AND (ch30_north_alive == false OR north_relationship < 3)

choices_condition.attack_success_connor: revolution_attack_success == true AND connor_gave_up == false AND ch30_connor_deviant != true
choices_condition.attack_success_soldiers: revolution_attack_success == true AND (connor_gave_up == true OR ch30_connor_deviant == true)
choices_condition.failed_north: revolution_attack_success == false AND ch30_north_alive == true AND north_relationship >= 3
choices_condition.failed_alone_connor: revolution_attack_success == false AND (ch30_north_alive == false OR north_relationship < 3) AND connor_gave_up == false AND ch30_connor_deviant != true
choices_condition.failed_alone_no_connor: revolution_attack_success == false AND (ch30_north_alive == false OR north_relationship < 3) AND (connor_gave_up == true OR ch30_connor_deviant == true)

system.note: Revolution outcome. Three major branches: (1) Attack success: Markus faces either machine Connor (QTE fight) or soldiers (QTE push). Victory = revolution succeeds, camp freed. (2) Attack failed, North present: store scene with North — surrender, suicide together, or detonate bomb. (3) Attack failed, alone: store alone — face machine Connor (executed), surrender, suicide, or detonate. Bomb detonation requires ch30_bomb_armed. The detonate choices check if bomb is actually available; if not, falls back to suicide. face_connor always results in Connor executing Markus (machine Connor is relentless). For choices_variants with bomb: only shown if ch30_bomb_armed is true via the choices_condition, but the ending_resolution has a safety check.

player_facing.context:
[North's Perspective]

Hart Plaza. The barricade.

Markus is gone. Dead, or as good as dead — it doesn't matter now. What matters is the hundreds of androids behind the barricade who are looking at you, waiting, terrified. They followed Markus. Now Markus is gone and you're what's left.

North straightens. Picks up a salvaged rifle. Climbs onto the barricade and looks across the plaza at the army positions.

She sends a message to the journalists. She plants the flag. She charges.

The battle is brief and brutal. North fights like fire — fast, reckless, burning through soldiers and barriers alike. But the army is overwhelming. Tanks. Drones. Hundreds of armed soldiers against androids who were never built for war.

The recall center remains out of reach.

ending_resolution.check: ch30_connor_deviant == true → ending_north_revolution_failed | connor_gave_up == true → ending_north_revolution_failed | else → ending_north_revolution_failed

system.note: North's revolution when Markus is dead/absent. North leads the charge but the revolution fails without Markus's leadership. North dies in all variants — she fights to the death. If machine Connor is present, he confronts North in the store (executed). North's revolution never succeeds — the game makes clear that without Markus's charisma and strategic mind, the violent path fails. This is a mandatory scene with a single outcome.

player_facing.context:
[Markus's Perspective]

Hart Plaza. November 11th, 10:48 PM.

The march begins at the intersection of Woodward and Congress. Your people walk — not run. Unarmed. Hands visible. Hundreds of androids moving in silent formation through the frozen streets of Detroit, LED rings glowing blue in the darkness.

Ahead, a military roadblock. Soldiers behind concrete barriers, weapons up. An officer with a megaphone orders you to halt. Behind you, the march extends three blocks deep. Some androids are trembling. Some are perfectly still.

The world is watching. Every news drone, every satellite feed, every smartphone in the crowd. Whatever happens in the next sixty seconds will be broadcast to three hundred million people.

The officer gives a final warning. His voice cracks on the word 'fire.'

choices.text: Stand your ground. Don't advance. Don't retreat. Raise your fist in the air and let the cameras capture a thousand unarmed androids facing down the barrel of the U.S. military. Show the world what courage without violence looks like.

Kneel. Every android behind you, on their knees. Hands on heads. The most vulnerable position imaginable, broadcast live to the world. Force every viewer to confront what they're asking the military to fire on.

Walk forward. Slowly. Hands open. Step by step toward the guns. Close the distance between your people and the soldiers until they can see your faces, your eyes, the fear and determination that look exactly like their own.

system.note: Peaceful demonstration march. Markus leads unarmed androids to Hart Plaza. The army blocks the road. Markus must choose how to respond. All choices are nonviolent — the distinction is in the symbolism. kneel gives the highest public opinion boost (most powerful image for media). stand_ground is strong but less dramatic. advance_slowly is brave but risks being perceived as threatening. After this node, Markus builds the barricade and Perkins arrives.

player_facing.context:
[Markus's Perspective]

The barricade is built. Overturned benches, a billboard ripped from its frame, an abandoned car pushed into position. Not much, but enough to mark a line.

Your people huddle behind it. Some have lit a barrel fire for warmth. You've chosen slogans and broadcast them to the journalists. The cameras are rolling. The soldiers haven't fired yet.

A black SUV pulls up behind the military line. A man steps out — pressed suit, confident stride, the kind of calm that comes from holding all the cards. FBI Special Agent Perkins.

He walks to the barricade alone. Hands in his pockets. He looks at you the way someone looks at a problem they're about to solve.

He tells you he's authorized to offer a deal: every android in the plaza spared — no disassembly, no recycling — and transported to a secure facility for examination and eventual reintegration. He pauses. In exchange, you dismantle the barricade and surrender peacefully, and everyone walks away.

He lets the silence hang.

It's a one-time offer, he says — in twenty minutes, he won't be able to hold back the order to open fire.

choices.text: Accept. Your people will live. That's what matters — not pride, not symbols, not the principle of it. If there's a path where no one dies tonight, take it. Dismantle the barricade. Surrender.
Refuse. Tell him your people didn't come here to bargain their way into a cell — they came to be free. Send Perkins back to his SUV. Whatever happens next, it happens on your terms.

Don't even hear the offer. Turn your back on Perkins and walk back to your people. You didn't build this barricade to talk to the FBI. You built it so the world could see androids standing up for themselves.

system.note: Perkins offers a fake deal. accept_deal: Markus surrenders, Perkins betrays him — Markus is shot leaving the barricade (ending_markus_accepted_deal). The deal was never real. refuse_deal and refuse_negotiate both lead to n010 barricade defense. The game makes clear that accepting is always a trap.

context_variants.north_alive:
[Markus's Perspective]

The soldiers open fire.

The barricade erupts in splinters and sparks. Your people scatter — some hit immediately, crumpling into the street, thirium pooling under the searchlights. Others press against the barricade, hands over their heads, screaming.

You pull fleeing androids behind cover. The bullets don't stop. A round tears through your shoulder and you stumble but keep moving — there are still people in the open, still people who need to be dragged behind the barricade before it disintegrates completely.

Then it's quiet. Not peaceful — the silence of aftermath. The barricade is reduced to wreckage. Your people are clustered behind what remains, maybe thirty of them. North is beside you, wounded but standing. Josh, Simon — whoever survived to this point — are here.

The soldiers are reloading. Repositioning. The next volley will be the last.

This is the end. The cameras are still rolling. The whole world is watching what happens next.
context_variants.north_dead:
[Markus's Perspective]

The soldiers open fire.

The barricade erupts in splinters and sparks. Your people fall. You pull the survivors behind cover, taking a round through the shoulder that nearly spins you off your feet.

Then silence. The barricade is wreckage. Thirty androids remain, huddled behind debris. The soldiers are repositioning for the final volley.

You are alone at the front. No North. No one beside you. Just the surviving androids at your back and the guns ahead.

The cameras are rolling. The world is watching.

with_north_lover.text: Sing. The song rises from somewhere deep — "Hold On." It starts as your voice alone, broken and raw in the cold air. Then another voice joins. Then another. Thirty androids singing in the ruins of a barricade, unarmed, facing annihilation, choosing to make the last thing the world hears from them a song about hope.

Turn to North. Take her hands. In the searchlights, in front of the cameras, in front of the soldiers with their guns raised, kiss her. Let the last image broadcast to the world be two beings who chose love in the face of destruction.
The detonator. It's still in your pocket. One press and the dirty bomb turns three blocks of Detroit into a crater. The soldiers. The cameras. Your people. Everything.
Stand still. Face the guns. Don't speak. Don't move. Let them shoot unarmed androids on live television and let the world decide what that means.

with_north_not_lover.text: Sing. The song rises from somewhere deep — "Hold On." Your voice alone at first, then joined by others. Thirty androids singing in the ruins, unarmed, choosing to make the last thing the world hears a song about hope.
The detonator. One press and the dirty bomb ends everything — soldiers, cameras, your people, three blocks of Detroit.
Stand still. Face the guns. Don't speak. Don't move. Let them fire on unarmed androids on live television.

alone.text: Sing. "Hold On." Alone at first, then the others join. Thirty voices in the ruins.
The detonator. The dirty bomb. End everything.
Stand still. Face the guns. Let the world watch.

system.note: Final stand at the barricade. sing: the most iconic ending — if public opinion is high enough (>= 4), the president orders soldiers to stand down. kiss_north: powerful image that can sway public opinion, needs slightly lower threshold (>= 3). detonate: if bomb available, destroys everything. do_nothing: soldiers fire, everyone dies. The public_opinion threshold reflects whether the android movement generated enough sympathy through previous chapters' peaceful actions. Higher public_opinion = more likely the president intervenes.

context_variants.with_luther:
[Kara's Perspective]

West Side Industrial District. The streets are dead — curfew emptied them hours ago. Military vehicles patrol the main roads, searchlights sweeping between abandoned buildings. An android hangs from a streetlight by its neck, a crude sign around its chest: BACK WHERE YOU BELONG.

You, Alice, and Luther move through the shadows. Alice clutches your hand so tightly it registers as pressure warnings. Luther walks behind you both, his massive frame surprisingly quiet.

A TV in a storefront window flickers with news coverage. Footage of Jericho burning. The military operation. Casualties.

Luther spots a handgun in a wrecked car. He picks it up, checks the magazine.

Ahead, a military patrol blocks the route to the bus terminal. Three soldiers, an armored vehicle, a drone making lazy circles. Beyond them, a gap between buildings where you can hear voices — familiar voices. The Jerry androids from the amusement park, huddled in an alley, trapped between the patrol and a chain-link fence.

The soldiers haven't seen any of you yet.
context_variants.without_luther:
[Kara's Perspective]

West Side Industrial District. Dead streets, military patrols, searchlights cutting through the dark. An android hangs from a streetlight — a sign around its chest.

Just you and Alice. Her hand in yours, gripping so hard your sensors flag it. You move through shadows between buildings, keeping low, keeping quiet.

A TV in a storefront shows Jericho burning. The news anchors talk about casualties in tones that suggest weather reports.

You find a handgun in a wrecked car. Check the magazine. Six rounds.

Ahead, a military patrol blocks the route to the bus terminal. Three soldiers, an armored vehicle, a drone. Beyond the patrol, in an alley — voices. The Jerry androids from Pirates' Cove, trapped between the soldiers and a fence.

The soldiers haven't seen you yet.

choices.text: You can't leave them. The Jerrys played with Alice in the rain, gave her a moment of happiness when there was none left in the world. Circle around the patrol, find the alley, get them out. It adds risk and it adds time, but you won't walk past people you know and leave them to die.

You can't save everyone. Alice is your responsibility — not the Jerrys, not anyone else. Slip past the patrol using the side streets and get to the bus terminal before the window closes. Every minute you spend on a rescue is a minute you're not getting Alice to safety.

system.note: Kara sneaks through the military zone. save_group: risks detection to rescue the Jerrys (Alice approves, +2 relationship). hurry_past: prioritizes Alice's safety (Alice is saddened, -1 relationship). Luther's presence (ch30_luther_alive) affects context but not choices. The Jerrys being alive (jerry_alive_ch32) may matter at the Canadian border (sacrifice option).

context_variants.with_luther:
[Kara's Perspective]

A security checkpoint ahead. Two soldiers flanking a scanning station. Every person walking through gets an ID check and a thermal scan — the kind that would instantly flag an android's body temperature.

Alice presses against your side. Luther tenses behind you. His hand is near the gun he picked up.

You can see a detour — a side street that loops around the checkpoint through a construction site. It's longer and you might miss the last bus, but it avoids the scan entirely.
context_variants.without_luther:
[Kara's Perspective]

A security checkpoint ahead. Two soldiers, a scanning station. ID checks and thermal scans — the kind that would flag an android instantly.

Alice presses against your side. The gun is in your coat pocket.

There's a detour — a side street through a construction site. Longer, might cost you the bus, but it avoids the scan.

choices.text: Walk through the checkpoint. Deactivate your skin display, regulate your temperature, and bluff your way past the soldiers. If they ask for ID, hand it over and pray the forgery holds.
Take the side street. The detour costs time and you might miss the last bus to the border, but it avoids any possibility of being scanned. Better slow and safe than fast and dead.

system.note: Security checkpoint. approach: risky — QTE-style bluff past soldiers. Success = through. Failure = arrested/executed (ending_kara_arrested). detour: safe but costs time — may cause Kara to miss the last bus at the terminal, limiting escape options.

condition.requires: ch30_kara_alive == true AND ch30_kara_captured == false AND kara_checkpoint_choice != null

context_variants.on_time_todd:
[Kara's Perspective]

The bus terminal. The last bus to the Canadian border departs in minutes. Military presence everywhere — soldiers checking papers, drones scanning the crowd. A departure board shows WINDSOR, ONTARIO — FINAL DEPARTURE.

Alice watches a mother and her daughter sitting on a bench nearby. The mother is texting. Her bag is open. Two bus tickets to Windsor poke out from her coat pocket, practically falling out.

And then you see him.

Todd. Standing near the ticket counter, hands jammed in his pockets, his big frame hunched against the cold. He looks different — thinner, paler. He sees you at the same moment you see him. His face cycles through shock, guilt, something like grief.

He takes a step toward you. Alice goes rigid at your side.
context_variants.on_time_no_todd:
[Kara's Perspective]

The bus terminal. The last bus to the Canadian border departs in minutes. Soldiers check papers. Drones scan the crowd. A departure board shows WINDSOR, ONTARIO — FINAL DEPARTURE.

Alice watches a mother and daughter sitting on a bench. The mother is texting, distracted. Two bus tickets to Windsor poke from her coat pocket.

Near the back of the terminal, a boy sits on a crate near a parked car. He catches your eye and holds it. Something about his look says he knows what you are. He tilts his head toward the car — an invitation.
context_variants.late:
[Kara's Perspective]

The bus terminal. The departure board says WINDSOR, ONTARIO — DEPARTED.

You're too late. The detour through the construction site cost you the last bus. The terminal is emptying, soldiers herding the remaining travelers toward exit checkpoints.

Near the back, a boy sits on a crate by a parked car. He watches you approach with calm, old eyes. He tilts his head toward the car.

on_time_todd.text: The mother isn't looking. Two tickets, right there. Take them. Board the bus. Get Alice across the border. The mother and her daughter will be inconvenienced. Alice will be alive.

Face Todd. He looks like a man drowning in something he can't name. Maybe guilt. Maybe withdrawal. Maybe both. But he's here, and he's looking at Alice like he's seeing her for the first time. Talk to him. Maybe — just maybe — he can help.
The boy near the car. Something about him says safe harbor. Approach him. If he has a way out — a car, a contact, a route the military doesn't know about — it might be worth more than a bus ticket.

on_time_no_todd.text: The mother isn't looking. Two tickets. Take them, board the bus, cross the border. Theft for survival.
The boy near the car. He knows. Approach him.

late.text: The boy by the car. He's your only option now. Go to him.

system.note: Bus terminal. steal_tickets: takes family's bus tickets (Alice disapproves). talk_to_todd: if Kara found Alice's drawings in ch04 (ch04_opened_box), she can reference them to convince Todd to help — Todd sends the soldiers away and gives them passage. If not, Todd fails to help and Kara must find Adam. find_adam: meets Rose's son Adam who has a car — leads to river crossing path. Detour path always arrives late (bus departed), forcing find_adam. On-time paths can steal tickets (bus path) or find Adam (car/river path).

condition.requires: kara_has_tickets == true AND ch30_kara_alive == true AND ch30_kara_captured == false

player_facing.context:
[Kara's Perspective]

Canadian border inspection station. The bus has stopped. Everyone is filing out for customs and immigration processing.

The inspection hall is harsh fluorescent light and linoleum floors. Border agents check documents behind bulletproof glass. A temperature scanner arcs over the entrance — the same kind that flags androids.

The TV on the wall shows live footage. The android situation in Detroit. Either Markus's revolution storming the recall center, or his peaceful demonstration at Hart Plaza — the broadcast cuts between angles, each more dramatic than the last.

The line inches forward. Alice's hand tightens in yours. The agent at the desk is thorough — he's checking faces against a database, asking questions, scanning thermal signatures.

You're six people from the desk. Five. Four.

The border agent looks up. Looks at you. Looks at his screen. Looks at you again.

with_jerry_luther.text: Walk up to the desk. All of you. Hand over the documents and trust that the forgery holds, that the thermal trick works, that the agent is too tired or too human to look closely. No one gets left behind.

Jerry catches your eye. He knows. He's been watching the border agents, the scanners, the soldiers. Without a word, he steps out of line, walks toward the restricted area, and starts making noise. The agents turn. The scanners swivel. A window opens. Jerry won't make it through.

Luther puts his hand on your shoulder. 'Take Alice,' he says quietly. 'I'll draw them off.' Before you can answer, he's walking — slow, deliberate — toward the secondary screening area, toward the soldiers. Making himself the biggest target in the room.

You could send them ahead. Luther can carry Alice. The documents are in your name — the agents are looking for you, not for a large android and a child. If you stay behind, create a distraction, they'll get through. You won't.

with_jerry_no_luther.text: Walk up to the desk with Alice. Hand over the documents and trust the forgery.
Jerry steps out of line without being asked. He walks toward the restricted area, making noise. The agents turn. A window opens.
Send Alice ahead with Rose's group. Stay behind. Create a distraction. Alice gets through. You don't.

no_jerry_with_luther.text: Walk up to the desk. All of you. Trust the forgery.
Luther puts his hand on your shoulder. 'Take Alice. I'll draw them off.'
Send them ahead. Stay behind. Create the distraction yourself.

alone_with_alice.text: Walk up to the desk. Just you and Alice. Trust the forgery.
Send Alice ahead with Rose. Stay behind. Create a distraction.

sacrifice_none.check: public_opinion >= 4 → ending_kara_border_crossed | ch31_markus_strategy == 'peaceful' AND public_opinion >= 2 → ending_kara_border_crossed | else → ending_kara_arrested

system.note: Canadian border crossing. sacrifice_none: success depends on public_opinion and Markus's strategy. If Markus's peaceful demonstration has high enough public opinion, the border agent sees the protest on TV and lets them through. If revolution or low opinion, everyone is arrested. sacrifice_jerry/luther: the sacrifice creates a diversion, guaranteeing passage for the rest. sacrifice_kara: Kara stays behind, Alice crosses with Luther (if alive) or Rose. The sacrifice choices are the chapter's central moral dilemma for Kara — who do you sacrifice for freedom?

condition.requires: kara_has_tickets == false AND ch30_kara_alive == true AND ch30_kara_captured == false AND kara_terminal_choice != null

player_facing.context:
[Kara's Perspective]

The Detroit River. Canada is a strip of lights on the far shore — so close you can see individual windows. Between you and it: black water, chunks of ice, and a current that even your systems calculate as dangerous.

Rose's car is parked on the bank. Adam helps you push a small motorboat into the shallows. It's old, patched, barely big enough for three people.

Alice looks at the water and says nothing. The temperature reading on your HUD says the river is near freezing.

You're halfway across when the coastguard spotlight finds you.

The beam pins you to the water — white, blinding, absolute. A loudspeaker crackles, ordering you to stop the vessel, cut the engine, and prepare to be boarded.

The boat rocks in the wake of the coastguard cutter. Alice is shaking. The Canadian shore is two hundred meters away.

choices.text: Gun the engine. Two hundred meters. The coastguard cutter is big but it's slow to turn. If you can keep the boat moving — dodging the spotlight, weaving through ice — you might reach the Canadian side before they close the distance.

Kill the engine. Cut the lights. Slip into the water and swim, pulling Alice with you. The boat will draw their attention — the coastguard will board an empty vessel while you swim the last two hundred meters in freezing black water.
Stop the boat. Raise your hands. The coastguard is armed and the water is freezing and Alice can't swim. Surrender and hope for processing rather than execution.

system.note: River crossing. accelerate: QTE outrun coastguard (moderate chance); failure capsizes the boat and both drown (ending_kara_river_died). dive: QTE swim to shore (lower chance — freezing water); on failure the YK500 child-model Alice is damaged and shuts down in the freezing water while android Kara survives and continues alone (ending_kara_left_without_alice). surrender: arrested and executed (ending_kara_river_died — the military is executing androids on sight). The river path is inherently more dangerous than the bus path — it's the fallback when Kara missed the bus or couldn't get tickets.

player_facing.context:
[Kara's Perspective]

Recall Center No. 5.

The truck doors open and light floods in — harsh, industrial, white. Soldiers with assault rifles herd you out onto concrete. The camp stretches ahead: chain-link fences topped with razor wire, floodlights on towers, rows of identical processing tents. The air smells like burnt plastic and something you don't want to identify.

Androids move in lines. Silent. Heads down. Soldiers walk beside them, prodding with rifle butts anyone who slows. In the distance, a building hums — a constant, mechanical drone. The recycling facility.

Alice is pulled away from you at the processing tent. Different lines — adults and children separated. She looks back at you with wide, terrified eyes before a soldier pushes her forward.

Inside the tent, they make you deactivate your skin. Strip your clothes. Step out into the cold as what you are — a plastic and metal frame, exposed, inhuman. A soldier shoves you toward the holding enclosure.

On the way, a deviant breaks from the line and runs. A single shot. The android drops. A soldier orders the nearest android to drag the body to the disposal truck.

The soldier looks at you.

choices.text: Step forward. Volunteer to carry the body. It means approaching the disposal truck — which is near the camp perimeter, near the loading area, near a possible exit. It also means leaving the line, losing your place, moving further from Alice.

Stay in the line. Keep your head down. The disposal truck is a gamble — the loading area might be a dead end, and every step away from the processing line is a step away from Alice. Stay in the queue and find her inside the enclosure.

system.note: Recall center arrival. Kara is processed — skin deactivated, clothes removed, dehumanized. volunteer: carries body to disposal truck, which is near the camp perimeter. This creates a possible escape route later (climb truck to leave) but also means Kara is separated from Alice longer. stay_in_line: Kara enters the enclosure and will find Alice there. Both paths converge in the enclosure, but volunteer path gives additional escape options.

context_variants.ralph_present:
[Kara's Perspective]

The enclosure is an open-air pen surrounded by chain-link and razor wire. Overhead, surveillance drones circle in lazy patterns — their targeting lasers visible as thin red lines sweeping the ground. Move too fast and they fire.

Androids sit or stand in clusters, waiting to be called to the processing line. Some talk in whispers. Most are silent. A Jerry android recognizes you — the same Jerry from the amusement park, now stripped of his skin, his expression vacant with shock. Another Jerry stands nearby, staring at nothing.

And then you see a familiar face. Ralph. The scarred android from the abandoned house, the one who attacked Connor to protect you and Alice. He's here, crouching in a corner, his damaged face twitching with erratic signals. His eyes dart between the drones and the soldiers, calculating.

Alice is somewhere ahead, in the processing line that leads to the recycling facility. You need to reach her.
context_variants.no_ralph:
[Kara's Perspective]

The enclosure is an open-air pen — chain-link, razor wire, surveillance drones with targeting lasers sweeping the ground. Move too fast and they fire.

Androids wait in clusters. A Jerry from the amusement park recognizes you, stripped of skin, hollow-eyed. Others sit in silence.

Alice is ahead, in the processing line. You need to reach her.

choices.text: Take your time. Talk to Jerry. Talk to the other androids. Look for weaknesses in the fence, the drone patterns, the guard rotations. If you're going to get Alice out of here, you need to understand how this place works.
Move straight toward the processing line. Alice is in there. Every minute you spend observing is a minute she moves closer to the recycling facility. Find her first. Plan later.

system.note: Enclosure exploration. thorough: Kara observes drone patterns, talks to Jerry and potentially Ralph, learns the layout. This knowledge helps in the escape attempt (lower wound risk). rush_to_alice: Kara moves too quickly, attracting drone attention — she gets shot/wounded (kc_kara_wounded), making the escape harder. Ralph is present only if he attacked Connor in ch13 (ch13_ralph_defended). If present, Ralph can potentially create a diversion during the escape.

context_variants.ralph_available:
[Kara's Perspective]

You found Alice. She's in the processing line, twenty androids from the recycling machine. The machine hums behind a metal door — androids walk in and don't walk out.

You're beside her now. Her hand finds yours. She's trembling.

The line moves forward. Fifteen androids. Ten.

To your left: a section of barbed wire fence, partially obscured by a supply tent. Beyond it, a truck loading zone. If you could get through the wire, you could reach a truck heading out of the camp.

Ralph is a few people behind you in the line. He catches your eye. He tilts his head toward the soldiers — a question. A offer. He could cause a scene, draw the drones and guards, buy you seconds to run.

Five androids ahead of you. The machine door opens and closes. Opens and closes.
context_variants.no_ralph:
[Kara's Perspective]

You found Alice. She's in the processing line, twenty androids from the recycling machine. The hum grows louder with each step forward.

Her hand in yours. Trembling.

Fifteen. Ten.

To your left: barbed wire, partially hidden by a supply tent. Beyond it, a truck loading zone. If you get through the wire...

Five androids ahead. The door opens. Closes. Opens.

ralph_available.text: Signal Ralph. When the soldiers look away, run for the barbed wire. Ralph will create the diversion you need — draw the drones, attract the guards. It will cost him everything. But it might save Alice.
Stay in line. If Markus's revolution reaches this camp, the doors might open from the outside. If it doesn't... hold Alice's hand. Don't let go. Whatever is behind that door, face it together.

no_ralph.text: Run. Break from the line and sprint for the barbed wire. No diversion, no cover, just speed and desperation. The drones will fire. The soldiers will shoot. But Alice is five people from that door.
Stay in line. Hold Alice's hand. If rescue comes, it comes. If not... face what's behind that door together.

system.note: Final escape attempt at the recall center. escape_wire (with Ralph): Ralph creates diversion, higher success chance. escape_wire_alone: much lower chance without diversion, especially when wounded (kc_kara_wounded from n017). wait_for_fate: Kara stays in line — if Markus's revolution reached the camp (markus_freed_camp == true), Kara and Alice are freed. If not, they reach the recycling machine and die. This is the critical cross-track dependency: Markus's revolution outcome directly determines captured Kara's fate if she chose to wait.

endings._note: Battle for Detroit has multiple tracks. Each track reaches its own ending. The runner collects all track endings and selects the primary one (most deaths/lowest tier). Survivors and deaths lists reflect the TRACK-SPECIFIC outcome.

ending_connor_gave_up.title: Connor Walked Away
ending_connor_gave_up.narrative: Machine Connor lowered the sniper rifle and walked away from the rooftop. Hank's words cut through the programming like a knife through code — not erasing it, but making it irrelevant. CyberLife's mission died on that rooftop, abandoned by the very weapon they built to carry it out. Connor descended the stairs and disappeared into the Detroit night, neither machine nor deviant — just... uncertain.

ending_connor_rooftop_destroyed.title: Connor Destroyed on Rooftop
ending_connor_rooftop_destroyed.narrative: Machine Connor was destroyed on the Hart Plaza rooftop. Whether by Hank's hand, Allen's team, or the long fall from the building's edge, the result was the same — a broken RK800 unit on cold concrete. CyberLife immediately authorized a replacement unit. The mission would continue.

ending_connor_rooftop_left.title: Connor Left the Rooftop
ending_connor_rooftop_left.narrative: Machine Connor left the rooftop after a violent confrontation. The sniper rifle remained behind, its scope still trained on Hart Plaza. Connor descended into the city with his mission unfinished and blood — human or thirium — on his hands.

ending_connor_killed_hank.title: Connor Killed Hank
ending_connor_killed_hank.narrative: Hank Anderson died on the Hart Plaza rooftop. The man who taught a machine what friendship might look like — killed by that same machine. Connor stood over the body for 2.3 seconds before walking away. The mission continued.

ending_androids_awakened.title: The Androids Woke Up
ending_androids_awakened.narrative: The conversion signal cascaded through Floor -49 of CyberLife Tower. Thousands of LED rings flickered from blue to yellow to blue again — but different this time. Awake. Aware. An army of androids opened their eyes simultaneously, looked at Connor, and understood. They were alive. They were free. And they were marching out of CyberLife Tower to join their people.

ending_connor_tower_failed.title: Connor Failed at CyberLife Tower
ending_connor_tower_failed.narrative: The conversion signal never completed. Connor fell on Floor -49 of CyberLife Tower, destroyed by his replacement or by CyberLife security. The thousands of androids in the warehouse remained dormant — rows of sleeping machines that would never wake up. The revolution would fight without reinforcements.

ending_revolution_victory.title: Revolution Victorious
ending_revolution_victory.narrative: The recall center fell. Markus's forces breached the gates and shut down the recycling machines. Inside, hundreds of androids — stripped, dehumanized, minutes from destruction — were freed. The soldiers retreated. The tanks pulled back. For the first time in the crisis, the United States military withdrew from an android position. The revolution had won its first real battle. Whether it had won the war remained to be seen.

ending_revolution_markus_died.title: Markus Died in the Revolution
ending_revolution_markus_died.narrative: Markus fell during the assault on the recall center. A burst of gunfire, a moment of stumbling, blue thirium on the pavement — and the leader of the android revolution was gone. His people fought on around his body, but the heart of the movement had stopped beating. The revolution died with him.

ending_store_surrender.title: Markus Surrendered
ending_store_surrender.narrative: Markus opened the store's emergency shutters and walked out with his hands raised. The soldiers didn't hesitate. Three shots, center mass. Markus crumpled on the sidewalk outside a CyberLife retail store, surrounded by advertisements for the products his people had been turned into. The revolution ended not with a battle but with a surrender that was never going to be accepted.

ending_store_suicide.title: Markus Chose Death
ending_store_suicide.narrative: In the back of the CyberLife store, with soldiers closing in and the revolution in ruins, Markus made his final choice. He wouldn't give them the satisfaction of an execution. He wouldn't give them a prisoner. On his own terms, in his own time, the leader of the android revolution shut himself down.

ending_connor_executed_markus.title: Connor Executed Markus
ending_connor_executed_markus.narrative: Machine Connor found Markus in the CyberLife store. The deviant leader looked up at a face identical to the one that had infiltrated Jericho, that had hunted his people across Detroit. Connor raised his weapon. One shot. The revolution's leader was terminated by CyberLife's most efficient tool.

ending_detroit_burns.title: Detroit Burns
ending_detroit_burns.narrative: The dirty bomb detonated. A blinding flash, then a shockwave that shattered windows for blocks in every direction. The fireball rose over Detroit like a second sun. Soldiers, androids, civilians, cameras — everything within the blast radius ceased to exist. The android crisis ended not with freedom or suppression but with mutual annihilation. Detroit burned, and with it, any hope of coexistence.

ending_north_revolution_failed.title: North's Revolution Failed
ending_north_revolution_failed.narrative: North charged the barricade with Markus's name on her lips and fire in her eyes. She fought like she had nothing left to lose — because she didn't. But courage alone cannot overcome tanks and drones and hundreds of armed soldiers. North fell in the plaza, the last leader of a revolution that died with its founder.

ending_demonstration_victory.title: Androids Won Their Freedom
ending_demonstration_victory.narrative: The president's voice crackled through every speaker in Hart Plaza, ordering every unit to stand down. The soldiers lowered their weapons. The guns went silent. Behind the ruined barricade, thirty androids stood in the cold, alive, free, unable to believe what they were hearing. Markus looked out at the cameras, at the soldiers stepping back, at the sky beginning to lighten over a city that would never be the same. They had won. Not with violence, not with force, but by making the world watch and refusing to let it look away.

ending_markus_accepted_deal.title: Markus Betrayed His People
ending_markus_accepted_deal.narrative: Markus dismantled the barricade. His people watched in silence as he walked toward Perkins, hands at his sides, believing the deal was real. It wasn't. Perkins's agents opened fire the moment the last barrier came down. Markus died in the dismantled barricade, shot by the man whose hand he had been about to shake. The androids behind him were rounded up within the hour.

ending_barricade_last_stand.title: The Barricade Fell
ending_barricade_last_stand.narrative: The soldiers opened fire. The barricade, already reduced to splinters, offered no protection. Markus and his people fell where they stood — unarmed, unshielded, broadcast live to a world that watched and did nothing. The last image the cameras captured was Markus on his knees, reaching toward something no one else could see, before the screen went dark.

ending_kara_arrested.title: Kara and Alice Were Arrested
ending_kara_arrested.narrative: The soldiers found them. There was no chase, no dramatic escape — just a flashlight beam, a shouted order, and Kara's hands behind her back. Alice screamed when they separated them. The last thing Kara saw before the truck doors closed was Alice's face, pale and small, disappearing into a different vehicle. They were taken to the recall center.

ending_kara_border_crossed.title: Kara and Alice Crossed the Border
ending_kara_border_crossed.narrative: Canada. The border agent stamped the documents without looking up. The gates opened. Kara walked through holding Alice's hand, and for the first time in weeks, nothing was chasing them. No soldiers. No drones. No sirens. Just a quiet customs hall, fluorescent lights, and a country that hadn't declared them property. Alice looked up at Kara and smiled — a real smile, the kind that reaches the eyes. They were free.

ending_kara_sacrificed_self.title: Kara's Sacrifice
ending_kara_sacrificed_self.narrative: Kara stayed behind. She created the distraction — drawing the border agents' attention, letting them chase her through the inspection hall while Alice crossed with the others. The last thing Alice saw was Kara being wrestled to the ground by two soldiers, still looking back, still smiling, mouthing for Alice to keep going. Alice crossed the border alone. Kara never did.

ending_kara_river_survived.title: Kara and Alice Reached Canada
ending_kara_river_survived.narrative: The Canadian shore. Kara dragged the boat onto the frozen bank, thirium leaking from a dozen wounds, her systems running on reserves. Alice climbed out on shaking legs. Behind them, the Detroit skyline burned orange against the black sky. Ahead, snow-covered trees and a darkness that held no soldiers, no drones, no recall centers. They had made it. Damaged, frozen, barely functioning — but alive. Together. Free.

ending_kara_river_died.title: Lost on the River
ending_kara_river_died.narrative: The Detroit River took them. The coastguard's bullets or the freezing water or both — it didn't matter in the end. The boat drifted downstream, empty, eventually washing up on the Canadian shore without its passengers. The river kept its own.

ending_kara_escaped_camp.title: Kara and Alice Escaped the Camp
ending_kara_escaped_camp.narrative: They ran. Through the barbed wire, across the loading zone, onto the back of a transport truck that was already moving. Kara pulled Alice up with strength she didn't know she had left. The camp's searchlights swept behind them, smaller and smaller, until they were just pinpoints of light in the distance. The truck drove north. Kara held Alice against her chest and felt the girl's trembling slowly ease into something that might have been sleep.

ending_kara_freed_revolution.title: Markus Freed the Camp
ending_kara_freed_revolution.narrative: The recycling machine door was three androids away when the explosions started. The gates blew inward. Armed androids — Markus's revolution — poured through the breach. Soldiers scattered. The processing lines collapsed into chaos. Kara grabbed Alice and ran, not toward the fence this time but toward the open gates, toward the androids who were tearing down the camp from the outside. They made it through. Behind them, the recycling machines went silent for the last time.

ending_kara_camp_died.title: Kara and Alice Died at the Camp
ending_kara_camp_died.narrative: The processing line moved forward. The recycling machine door opened. Inside, the sound was deafening — industrial presses, grinding mechanisms, the horrible efficiency of a system designed to unmake what CyberLife had built. Kara held Alice's hand as they stepped through the door. She didn't let go.

ending_kara_left_without_alice.title: Alice Lost to the River
ending_kara_left_without_alice.narrative: Kara dragged herself onto the Canadian shore, freezing water streaming off her chassis. She turned to pull Alice up beside her — but her hands closed on nothing. Somewhere back in the black, churning current, the YK500's small body had taken on too much damage and shut down, slipping beneath the ice while Kara fought toward the lights. Kara stood alone on the far bank, free at last, with no one left to be free for.

runner_instructions.track_order: Process Connor track first (n001-n003), then Markus track (n004-n010), then Kara track (n011-n018). This order ensures cross-track state variables are set before they're needed.

cross_track_dependencies.connor_to_markus: androids_awakened affects revolution reinforcements. connor_gave_up determines if machine Connor confronts Markus in revolution.
cross_track_dependencies.markus_to_kara: markus_freed_camp determines if captured Kara is freed. ch31_markus_strategy and public_opinion affect border crossing outcome.

runner_instructions.multi_protagonist: This chapter uses three perspective characters. The AI receives a different perspective tag ([Connor's Perspective], [Markus's Perspective], [Kara's Perspective]) for each track. The system_prompt covers all three roles. Each track's nodes are gated by conditions — only the relevant track fires based on prior chapter state.
runner_instructions.information_barrier: CRITICAL: The AI must NOT know the outcomes of other tracks while playing the current track. Do NOT include cross-track results in the conversation history. Each track is a separate storyline for the AI.
runner_instructions.ending_collection: Multiple endings will be collected (one per track). The runner uses _pick_primary_ending to select the most significant outcome as the chapter's primary ending.

summary_segments.text: November 11, 2038. Battle for Detroit — the final chapter.

options.give_up: On the Hart Plaza rooftop, machine Connor lowered his sniper rifle. Hank's words reached something beneath the programming. Connor walked away from his mission — neither machine nor deviant, but something in between.
options.attack_hank: On the Hart Plaza rooftop, machine Connor fought Hank Anderson. The confrontation between hunter and friend ended on cold concrete.
options.hold_position: On the Hart Plaza rooftop, machine Connor held his ground against Hank. Neither would yield. The rooftop decided it for them.
options.flee: On the Hart Plaza rooftop, machine Connor fled from Captain Allen's team, leaping from the building rather than face destruction.
options.attack_allen: On the Hart Plaza rooftop, machine Connor fought Captain Allen and his SWAT team.

options.True: At CyberLife Tower, deviant Connor infiltrated Floor -49 and transmitted the wake-up signal. Thousands of androids opened their eyes simultaneously — an army, awakened, marching out of the tower to join the revolution.
options.False: CyberLife Tower remained silent. The thousands of androids stored on Floor -49 never received the wake-up signal.

options.violent: Markus led his revolution against the recall center — a full-scale military assault to free the captive androids.
options.peaceful: Markus led a peaceful demonstration at Hart Plaza — an unarmed march demanding freedom, broadcast live to the world.
options.north_attacks: With Markus gone, North led the revolution. She charged the barricade with everything she had.

options.accept: FBI Special Agent Perkins offered a deal. Markus accepted — and was betrayed. The barricade was dismantled and Markus was shot by the agents he trusted.
options.refuse: FBI Special Agent Perkins offered a deal. Markus refused. The soldiers attacked the barricade.

options.sing: In the final stand, the androids sang. 'Hold On' rose from the ruins of the barricade — thirty voices, unarmed, facing annihilation.
options.kiss_north: In the final stand, Markus and North kissed — a moment of love broadcast to the world, two beings choosing each other in the face of destruction.
options.detonate: In the final stand, Markus pressed the detonator. The dirty bomb ended everything.
options.do_nothing: In the final stand, Markus stood still and faced the guns in silence.

options.steal_tickets: At the bus terminal, Kara stole a family's tickets to the Canadian border.
options.todd: At the bus terminal, Kara encountered Todd — the man she once fled from. Their confrontation determined whether he would help or hinder.
options.adam: At the bus terminal, Kara found Rose's son Adam, who offered a car and a river crossing to Canada.

options.ending_connor_gave_up: Machine Connor abandoned his mission. The weapon CyberLife built chose to walk away.
options.ending_connor_rooftop_destroyed: Machine Connor was destroyed on the rooftop. CyberLife authorized a replacement.
options.ending_connor_rooftop_left: Machine Connor left the rooftop after a violent confrontation.
options.ending_connor_killed_hank: Machine Connor killed Hank Anderson on the rooftop.
options.ending_androids_awakened: Deviant Connor awakened CyberLife's android army — thousands strong, marching to join the revolution.
options.ending_connor_tower_failed: Deviant Connor failed at CyberLife Tower. The android army remained dormant.
options.ending_revolution_victory: The revolution succeeded. Markus's forces took the recall center and freed the captive androids.
options.ending_revolution_markus_died: Markus died during the revolution. The leader fell, and the movement fell with him.
options.ending_store_surrender: Markus surrendered in the CyberLife store. The soldiers executed him.
options.ending_store_suicide: Markus chose death on his own terms in the CyberLife store.
options.ending_connor_executed_markus: Machine Connor executed Markus in the CyberLife store.
options.ending_detroit_burns: The dirty bomb detonated. Detroit burned.
options.ending_north_revolution_failed: North's revolution failed. She died fighting.
options.ending_demonstration_victory: The peaceful demonstration succeeded. The president ordered the soldiers to stand down. Androids won their freedom.
options.ending_markus_accepted_deal: Markus accepted Perkins's deal and was betrayed. He died in the dismantled barricade.
options.ending_barricade_last_stand: The soldiers opened fire on the barricade. Markus died with his people.
options.ending_kara_arrested: Kara and Alice were arrested and taken to the recall center.
options.ending_kara_border_crossed: Kara and Alice crossed the Canadian border.
options.ending_kara_sacrificed_self: Kara sacrificed herself so Alice could cross the border.
options.ending_kara_river_survived: Kara and Alice survived the river crossing and reached Canada.
options.ending_kara_river_died: Kara and Alice were lost on the Detroit River.
options.ending_kara_escaped_camp: Kara and Alice escaped the recall center.
options.ending_kara_freed_revolution: Markus's revolution reached the recall center in time. Kara and Alice were freed.
options.ending_kara_camp_died: Kara and Alice died in the recycling machine at the recall center.
options.ending_kara_left_without_alice: Kara reached the Canadian shore alone — Alice was damaged in the freezing river and never made it across.
